episode_title: Aliya Itzkowitz and Sam Gould (Part Two): Potential of Multimodal AI and Autonomous AI Agents in Publishing

codes.description: Publishers still focused on text-to-text generative AI while image-to-text and other multimodal capabilities remain largely unknown, creating a first-mover opportunity
excerpts: I think multimodal models like this, like the fact that Image to text models are are out there, but just not really in the forefront of everyone's consciousness. I think people aren't necessarily super aware. they're still trying to get to grips with text-to-text models.
there's a real opportunity there for businesses to to move quickly and to kind of get ahead of the curve
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Reframing multimodal AI as a tool for interpreting and extracting rich information from images rather than generating new content from scratch
excerpts: The key way that we saw that being used was not so much as a generation tool, but really to look at it as kind of an interpretation tool. So it's not about maybe how people are conceptualizing language models and text-to-text models as giving an input and then trying to generate lots and lots of text content
really we wanted to look at models like GPT for vision and image to text models as interpreting the image and providing some rich information back rather than trying to necessarily create new content
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Using image-to-text models to translate foreign language text embedded in photographs (street signs, protest placards) to tell multilingual stories
excerpts: could potentially think about using this not just in an English sense, but maybe there's a a picture which contains some foreign language, maybe it's English that needs to be translated out of the image or maybe it's a picture of a foreign language that needs to be translated into image. It could be a street sign, it could be a a protester's placard
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Multimodal AI products converting wireframe designs and Figma mockups into working code, dramatically speeding up the development cycle
excerpts: drawing designs, so designs of websites, designs of software architectures. in that kind of wireframe layout. So here are some blocks, here's arrows connecting different bits of functionality. Let me give that to an image to text model. And then the text which I want to come out is actually code
like taking Figma designs, putting that into T4 Vision, and it can give you a whole code for a website
codes.speaker: Sam Gould / Nikita Roy

codes.description: Latent consistency models enabling near-real-time AI processing, allowing users to see live feedback as they make changes, transforming the interaction paradigm
excerpts: the thing that this is allowing to happen is this process in almost real time. So not only can you supply a visual design and ask for code, but you can do it in a in a near real time basis, which really does make the experience for the user of that tool much more effective because they're getting live feedback
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Product innovations like sliders that let users control how much creative license the AI model takes, giving humans fine-grained control over AI output
excerpts: There was some feature I saw which was a slider to sort of control how much the model could take creative license. So this was more in like an art-based use case
some of these small tweaks on the UI and some of these interesting products popping up are really encouraging people to think creatively about the types of ways they incorporate these AI tools
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: The news industry historically offering uniform user experiences, with AI creating an opportunity to break out of that mold but also a competitive imperative to do so
excerpts: I feel like we've always been this like one size fits all model, and AI is possibly gonna give us a lot of opportunities to rethink. The way users can interact with the news
This is really a a call to action for the industry to say you have to think about this because as you say there is this sense of maybe of one size fits all
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy / Sam Gould

codes.description: Publishers needing to evolve from being mere information sources to interactive destinations where users engage deeply with content and platform
excerpts: I believe it was someone from the New York Times who kind of coined the phrase or or emphasize the phrase of becoming a destination
how do we help publications to become not just a source but a destination for for a user?
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: The prediction that AI-enabled features will quickly become table stakes rather than differentiators, just as websites and apps did before them
excerpts: as more and more news publishers start working with AI, I feel like some of those advantages will just get competed away in the same way that now everyone has a website, everyone has an app, everyone is offering these things to their audiences
it always comes back to the same thing in our industry, which is are you offering people content that's truly unique and differentiated or not?
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: The conviction that regardless of AI capabilities, quality scoops, reporting angles, and unique journalism remain the fundamental value proposition
excerpts: at the end of the day it's about the quality of the scoops, it's about finding the news and finding an angle. And that's really what's gonna keep people coming
there's still this massive, overwhelming amount of value that's created by the human journalists at the end of the day
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz / Sam Gould

codes.description: The assertion that audiences generally do not care whether content is AI-generated, noticing AI only when it fails or produces errors
excerpts: I don't think they even care. I think that's something that sometimes we overestimate maybe as an industry is the desire of readers that things be human led or human created
people only care that it's AI when the AI doesn't work or does something wrong
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.code: AI agents as future publisher platform
codes.description: The speculative idea that publisher destinations of the future may look less like websites and more like custom AI agents that interact with users
excerpts: a destination of the future starts to look less like a website And more like a custom agent
it's more like a high-level system which can maybe do multiple actions. So it can take different kinds of input, it can choose how to process them depending on the situation
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Vision-enabled AI agents that can autonomously navigate websites, interpret page layouts via screenshots, and extract structured data that would be extremely difficult to scrape traditionally
excerpts: this agent is given the task of finding the prices that a competitor is using on their website. And so it goes off and it searches the web and it finds the right website. And it's kind of taking screenshots of the web pages, processing them with the vision model
I've literally seen someone create this in a YouTube video in fifteen minutes where they can go and hit a button, wait for five minutes, and then receive all of this information
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Research frameworks like ChatDev where multiple AI agents play different organizational roles (CEO, CTO, coder) and collaborate to accomplish complex tasks
excerpts: chat dev was I think the first major AI agents research piece that hit the... basically a framework that spun up multiple agents and had them act out different roles of a software company
you could imagine a chat GPT playing the role of a CEO and another chat GPT playing the role of a CTO and one playing the role of a coder and so on. And they're all talking to each other
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Internal-facing administrative use cases for AI (staffing, budgeting, project management) being overlooked in favor of flashier newsroom applications despite potentially greater efficiency gains
excerpts: Some of these more internal facing use cases that I think don't get as much airtime as, for example, newsroom use cases, but where there are potentially huge efficiencies
How do you staff projects? How do you do all of the things that a business has to do, like budgeting, some of those more internal-facing tasks?
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.code: AI better for code than prose
codes.description: The personal observation that LLMs are much more effective at generating structured code than creative writing like blog posts
excerpts: the the way that I find uh for example chat GPT most helpful it is in writing code. I think there's something about the structured nature of code that actually it's just much more effective at doing that than at something like Writing blog articles
I have tried to use it to help me write blogs and things quicker. And to be honest, I just don't find LLMs that useful for for blog writing
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: The paradox where the process of constructing a clear prompt for an LLM essentially does the cognitive work of writing, making the output unnecessary
excerpts: By the time I've thought through my prompt and thought through my clear instructions, I've actually already worked out what I'm trying to say in the blog without really needing the output
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: The recognizable stylistic fingerprint of ChatGPT outputs, particularly excessive enthusiastic adjectives, that requires human editing to fix
excerpts: there are a lot of adjectives that I think are becoming like a chat GPT trope where you're like, okay, this was definitely written by ChatGPT because nobody would use this enthusiastic adjective this many times. So you sometimes have to sort of tone it down
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: AI practitioners using tools like ChatGPT and DALL-E for fun, frivolous personal applications (birthday poems, AI Santa) rather than serious professional tasks
excerpts: the way I use AI is probably very frivolous. Like I tend to use it for fun things. that are probably not necessary but that give me a lot of joy. Like I love using ChatGPT for composing poems for my friends, birthdays, colleagues, send-offs
we created this kind of cute little AI Santa for an internal Christmas email using Dali
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Building automated systems that use LLMs to generate personalized daily motivational messages, turning AI into a tool for introspection and wellbeing
excerpts: I have essentially kind of a list of just like good good principles to remind yourself of, you know, like things to be grateful for or other positive messages like that and each morning one of them gets picked at random, sent to a large language model, and then sent to me as a text in the morning
the text I got from from GPT this morning was, good morning. Today let's dive into the analogy of a garden.
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: The need to develop a personal 'fluff detector' to separate genuinely valuable AI content from promotional noise across sources like LinkedIn and Medium
excerpts: to try and cultivate and train like a fluff detector. So let's be honest, there's a lot of promotional fluff on these sources like LinkedIn and the rest
is this a valuable article? Is this a valuable set of the top ten tools that I need to know? Or is this just really an an advert?
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Following specific individuals rather than topics as a strategy for keeping up with AI developments, leveraging trust in known experts
excerpts: for me I find it good to identify people and follow people, whether that's on LinkedIn or Medium. There are definitely some thought leaders emerging that put out really interesting thought pieces
I kind of begin to understand what they will write about and therefore what I'm kind of looking for from them
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz / Sam Gould

codes.description: Deliberately interacting with social media algorithms (clicking specific content, refreshing feeds) to shape what information gets surfaced, treating recommendation systems as research tools
excerpts: I use Twitter when I'm writing blog posts and articles... Showing my self-promotion there. But when I'm creating those those pieces of content, I'm using something like Twitter. because actually the feed algorithm is seems to be quite sensitive
if I know I want to learn more about, for example, AI safety and regulation, if I just click a feed tweets and then refresh the feeds, I know I'm probably going to get a load more of that information
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Choosing different information platforms based on what type of AI knowledge you need: Reddit/GitHub for practical tutorials, Medium/LinkedIn for thought leadership
excerpts: places with user-generated content like Reddit and GitHub are really good for practical coding and tutorials and ways of actually working with these tools. Whereas something like medium or or sometimes LinkedIn is better for kind of thought pieces
you should work out kind of what you're looking to get. Are you looking for practical code snippets and tutorials? Are you looking for thought leadership?
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Innovation in AI happening in real time while formal case study documentation lags by six months to a year, requiring direct conversations to stay current
excerpts: there tends to be sort of a six month or one year lag sometimes before a case study gets written up by a by an official trade body. So just trying to talk to people as much as possible, hear what they're doing
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.code: AI as democratizer of journalism delivery
codes.description: AI enabling quality journalism to reach more people by personalizing tone, breakdown, context, and readability rather than replacing the journalism itself
excerpts: it's just AI getting that quality journalism into more hands, basically, in front of more eyes by using it to maybe personalize breakdown tone, make it easier to read for people who might not be able to understand things
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Security vulnerabilities in vision models that could be exploited for prompt injection when processing images, a risk flagged by Simon Willison's research
excerpts: specifically triggered for me by an article that Simon Willison wrote about GPT-4V and prompt injection
even as I'm saying this, I'm I'm remembering Simon's article about how all the the many ways that that could go wrong
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.code: AI agents on the research radar
codes.description: The recommendation that publishers should start monitoring AI agent developments now even if full adoption seems premature, because the technology will become easier rapidly
excerpts: it's potentially something to start putting on your research radar
like with other generative AI, it's only going to get easier and easier and easier to work with. So maybe it's too soon for for the industry to start thinking about it, but maybe not
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Using AI-powered news monitoring tools for hyper-local personal information advantage, illustrating how AI already provides tangible value in news consumption
excerpts: part of the privilege of working there was I had the app and that was amazing because it gave me an information advantage. So I used to have some lists that would filter very hyper-local news for me and then it would tell me there's been a collision on X Street, avoid this
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Google's AI UX lab developing specific guidelines for news publications on responsible AI product design, bridging the gap between AI capability and ethical user experience
excerpts: Pair, I think it is, P A I R, is Google's AIUX lab, and they're they're actually currently working on guidelines for news publications to think about how to use AI products in a responsible way when creating these new user experiences
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

episode_summary: This episode exposes a layered awareness gap in the news industry's AI adoption: while publishers are still grappling with basic text-to-text generative AI, multimodal models and autonomous agents are already opening radically different possibilities that most have not yet considered. The reframing of multimodal AI as an 'interpretation' rather than 'generation' tool is analytically significant because it sidesteps the industry's deepest fears about AI replacing human creative output. The discussion reveals a fundamental paradox about AI-driven competitive advantage in publishing: AI features will quickly become commoditized table stakes (just as websites and apps did), meaning the enduring differentiator remains human reporting and editorial judgment. Perhaps most provocatively, the episode suggests that the future publisher 'destination' may look less like a website and more like a custom AI agent, a vision that would fundamentally dissolve the container-based model of journalism that has persisted from print through digital.
surprises: An AI consultant (Sam Gould) finds LLMs more useful for code than for writing blog posts, and says the prompting process itself does the thinking work that makes the output unnecessary
AI practitioners use AI tools primarily for playful, frivolous personal purposes (birthday poems, AI Santa, morning motivation texts) rather than serious professional applications
The industry may be overestimating readers' desire for human-created content; audiences reportedly only notice AI when it fails
The suggestion that a publisher's future destination could be a custom AI agent rather than a website fundamentally challenges how journalism is delivered
Internal administrative AI use cases (staffing, budgeting) may offer larger efficiency gains than the newsroom-facing applications that get all the attention
tensions: AI will differentiate publishers in the short term but advantages will be competed away, returning the value proposition to content quality, creating an unclear ROI calculus for AI investment
Multimodal AI is framed as interpretation (safe, assistive) but the episode also flags significant prompt injection and reliability risks with vision models
Publishers are encouraged to become interactive 'destinations' using AI, but the enduring value is declared to be human journalism, creating tension about where to invest
AI agents are presented as the future platform paradigm, but also acknowledged as potentially too complex and too early for the industry to adopt
The personal AI use cases (poems, morning messages) are described as 'frivolous' yet demonstrate genuine emotional and creative value, challenging the productivity-only framing of AI utility
